explanationHtml:
Step 1: Simplify each equation.
Equation 1: \(x + 6y = -14\)
Equation 2: divide by 5 \(\Rightarrow x + 6y = 0\)
Equation 3: divide by 2 \(\Rightarrow x + 6y = 19.5\)

Step 2: Compare slopes and intercepts.
All three have slope \(-\frac{1}{6}\) but different \(y\)-intercepts (\(-14/6\), \(0\), \(19.5/6\)), so they are three distinct parallel lines with no point in common.

Step 3: Test all four choices.

  • Choice A — ‘Zero’: Three parallel, non-coincident lines share no common point ✓
  • Choice B — ‘Exactly one’: A unique intersection requires at least two non-parallel lines; impossible here ✗
  • Choice C — ‘Exactly two’: A linear system can never have exactly two solutions ✗
  • Choice D — ‘Infinitely many’: Would require all three equations to be the same line; the constants \(-14, 0, 19.5\) differ ✗

That matches choice A — A is correct.
